captions.text: First of all, I admire the restraint on  display here; Saros is the kind of title
that most developers would've stuck a colon  and an extra subtitle onto. Like "Saros:
Eclipse of Man" or "Saros: Lords of Wombat" in a  classic example of what I've previously termed the
"Bollock Sandwich" variety of game title. But  it seems they resisted the temptation in this
case. It's an open-faced Bollock Sandwich at most.  Well done, Saros. "Thanks, Yahtz!" You're welcome,
Saros. "Probably doesn't bode well for us that  that's the note of praise you're opening with,
does it, Yahtz?" Ooh, we've got a smart one here,  boys and girls. Saros is the new game exclusive to
the PS5—gosh, it's almost quaint that such things  still exist, isn't it? More power to you, Sony;
you keep on fighting that console war by yourself  like a Vietnam veteran with dementia. We'll just
stand here carefully moving valuable ornaments out  of the range of your swinging fists—and comes to
us from the developer of Returnal, a triple-A  attempt at a classic looping roguelite formula
with influence from bullet hell shooters  where everything was slightly teal. Saros,
in contrast, is a triple-A attempt at a  classic looping roguelite formula with
influence from bullet hell shooters  where everything is slightly orange.
Guess we know Housemarque's retirement  plan. Saros follows Arjun Devraj,
a space soldier man whose name would earn a  very unreasonable number of points in Scrabble,
who has come to the distant planet of Carcosa  on a ship called Echelon IV to figure out what
happened to Echelons I, II, and III, with the  assumption that three entire spaceship crews,
who went to a hostile alien world at the behest  of an evil megacorporation for whom wellbeing of
individual personnel is so far down the priority  list it's rubbing up against the glossary,
probably don't cut communications because they're  having a really lovely time and checking in
with head office quite slipped everyone's minds  because they were busy learning to windsurf. No,
it turns out it's the usual thing: everyone's  dead and there's monsters and the ruins of a
mysterious alien civilization that nobody knew  about, so now Arjun has to figure out the whole
dealio. And there's still some spaces on the Bingo  board to knock out, so how about this one? Arjun's
also looking for his missing wife, who was on  one of the earlier ships, and you just know
from the way the plot opens in medias res with  Arjun's relationship with his wife not properly
established that he's in for a plot twist right to  the balls there, if not a full-on Isaac Clarking.
And of course Arjun's big, fat, oblivious ass  gets fairly swiftly killed only to find he
keeps getting conveniently resurrected at home  base for no clear reason. Thus is the roguelite
format enabled. So how does Saros differ from  Returnal besides having dramatically shifted
the hue slider? Well, for one thing, there's a  bit more of a character emphasis. Arjun has a
whole support team of fellow astronauts  at home base with whom his relationship
gradually breaks down as tensions rise over the  situation's utter hopelessness and him being all
weird and fixated about his possibly imaginary  wife. But in fairness to Arjun, his crewmates
are all about as much use as a blind squirrel  at a spot-the-difference competition. "Gosh,
it seems like everyone who comes to this  planet mysteriously goes insane and murders
everyone around them. Huh, better watch out  for that. On a completely unconnected note,
why's the pilot speaking in tongues  and drawing ominous pictures on the
walls in his own blood and shit? I'd take my  career more seriously if I were him." Man,
if this were a Dan O'Bannon screenplay, none of  you motherfuckers would survive past the staples.
So we march straight past these timewasters  and head on through the teleporter for another
run at whatever monster-haunted hellscape we're  currently working our way through. And the bullet
hell-inspired combat from Returnal is back, with  all the biomechanical monsters emitting streams
of colored balls in another example of what I'm  coming to think of as the Combat System Highway
Code because every kind of ball has a specific  response. If you see red balls, parry them. If
you see yellow balls, dodge them. If you see blue  balls, stop thinking about your missing wife so
much. Housemarque is an old hand developer  going back to the '90s who made basically
nothing but classic-style arcade shooters up until  Returnal, and while in the thick of the combat,
I'd frequently think to myself, "Boy, sure would  be nice if I had a classic-style arcade shooter
camera right now so I wasn't being blindsided by  all these highly mobile enemies constantly spewing
out bubbles like a van full of laundry detergent  crashing into a fireworks storage facility." The
format requires us to battle our way through the  current area and then attempt the boss fight,
but the areas are so bloody long. Get killed  and that's another 20 minutes plodding through
the same infested rooms before you even get  another chance to memorize the boss's patterns.
And then there's the guns you get. As you progress  through an area, new guns randomly drop that you
basically have to switch to because you need the  higher numbers to meet the increasing challenge,
but sometimes the gun sucks or doesn't  suit your playstyle or the situation,
so you'll be happily plugging away with  an assault rifle with homing bullets that
gently massage your prostate as they come out and  then two rooms before the boss get lumped with a
shotgun with one barrel aimed at your own foot and  a spread wider than a Saudi Arabian parking space,
so you just humiliate yourself for a  few minutes before hi-ho-home-you-go,
another half-hour of platforming challenges before  you get another pull on the slot machine. Yes,
the order of rooms gets randomized each time,  but they never feel different because the main
points are always the same. It's like shaking  up a bag of Skittles and telling me, "No,
really, there might be M&Ms in there this  time." So the end result feels less like
a roguelite and more like a standard  shooter with really unreasonably far
apart checkpoints. I was having particular  trouble with a boss in the game's back half,
and I'd attempted it enough times that the  NPCs in the home base ran out of shit to say.
This is no fuckin' Hades where the NPCs seemingly  always have new dialogue after a run even if you
apocalyptically suck at the game and they resort  to criticizing your flip flops. No, fail, like,
three times without making plot progress, and all  your dudes just mutely stare at you like puppies
in a shop window as you make another perfectly  straight death march from the spawn point to the
teleporter. And after that, I found myself going  into a trance. My conscious brain died. All the
repeated platforming challenges and brightly lit  ball-based combat started passing over me like
the fucking Stargate sequence at the end of 2001:  A Space Odyssey. With the NPCs no longer talking,
I soon completely forgot firstly what I was  trying to achieve at that point in the story,
then everything I'd done in the story up to  that point, then everything I'd done in my life,
generally, up to that point. I dissociated from  my body. My consciousness was smeared across
reality as the totality of my being was reduced  to eyes and fingertips, existing in a single,
infinite moment of this one fucking area and its  fucking boss fight. Then I beat it and reality
snapped back like a set of cheap suspenders  and made my nipples bleed. So in conclusion,
I can't recommend Saros because it  almost annihilated my sense of self.
And a ketamine overdose would've  at least had interesting scenery.
